replication_study.hypothesis: Among school students learning English as a foreign language, students who regularly speak a minority (non-instructional) language at home (bilingual group) will have higher English achievement than monolingual peers after controlling for age, gender, socio-economic status, parental education, and cultural capital, and excluding students who speak English at home.

data_plan.source_type: School-based language assessment dataset with plausible values for English reading, listening, and writing; background questionnaires
data_plan.wave_or_subset: Cross-national student sample across multiple countries (country_id), schools (school_id)
data_plan.sample_size: N=20026 students (47 columns; country_id, school_id, English PVs, background variables)
data_plan.unit_of_analysis: Individual student nested in school nested in country
data_plan.access_details: Provided in the replication_data folder of the task package; analysis will read from /app/data/Final replication dataset.rds

qualification.explanation: This dataset provides an independent sample of students learning English as a foreign language, with measures of English proficiency and home language use similar in spirit to the original study. It contains key background controls (age, gender, socio-economic status, parental education, cultural capital), allowing a conceptual replication of the core association between immigrant bilingualism and English achievement.
qualification.similarity_to_original: Both datasets are school-based assessments of English as a foreign language with group comparisons of bilingual vs monolingual students and adjustment for background characteristics. Original: ELEMENT (Berlin) sixth graders with English Cloze test scaled by Rasch to WLEs, controls include HISEI, parental education, books, and general cognitive abilities (original_paper.pdf; post_registration.json: data.source, method, models, control_variables). Replication: The provided RDS includes plausible values for English reading/listening/writing (PV1_READ, PV1_LIST, PV1_WRIT_C etc.), background variables HISEI, PARED, SQt21i01 (books at home), gender SQt01i01, and age I08_ST_A_S02A (Final replication dataset.rds: columns and summaries). Bilingual status is derivable from home language variable I03_ST_A_S26A; exclusion of students speaking English at home uses I03_ST_A_S27B (Replication code and dataset summaries).
qualification.deviation_from_original: Original used a Berlin-only sixth-grade cohort (ELEMENT), clustered by classes, multiple imputation, WLE-scaled English scores (M=100, SD=20), and controlled for general cognitive abilities and German reading proficiency (post_registration.json; original_paper.pdf 2.2–3.3). Replication data are cross-national (country_id present), with plausible values for multiple English dimensions (PV*_*), and do not include a general cognitive abilities measure or German L2 proficiency. The model will therefore be a three-level mixed model (country/school/student) instead of class-clustered models with MI, and we cannot control for general cognitive ability or German proficiency. We will document this as a conceptual replication with noted limitations.

data_plan.notes: Key fields present: I03_ST_A_S26A (home language for bilingual indicator), I03_ST_A_S27B (English at home exclusion), SQt01i01 (gender), I08_ST_A_S02A (age), HISEI (SES), PARED (parent education), SQt21i01 (books at home), PVs for English reading/listening/writing. No direct measure of general cognitive abilities or German reading proficiency. PV scaling differs from WLE (original). Multilevel structure spans schools and countries; original clustered by class. The replication code averages PVs to create an overall English score, and filters observations with positive test weights FSW_*_TR.

planned_method.steps:
1) Load /app/data/Final replication dataset.rds.
2) Construct bilingual indicator: bilingual=1 if I03_ST_A_S26A indicates use of a non-instructional/minority language at home (including mixed), else 0 for monolingual; exclude students with I03_ST_A_S27B==1 (speak English at home).
3) Compute English domain averages: ave_writing, ave_reading, ave_listening as means of PV1–PV5 per domain; compute overall average_english as mean of ave_writing, ave_reading, ave_listening.
4) Recode books-at-home (SQt21i01) to a numeric cultural capital scale and standardize (Z_Cultural); center HISEI (c_HISEI) and age (c_age); standardize parental education (Z_Parental). Encode gender from SQt01i01 as factor.
5) Fit a 3-level linear mixed-effects model: average_english ~ bilingual + gender + c_age + c_HISEI + Z_Parental + Z_Cultural + (1 | country_id/school_id).
6) Obtain model summary, marginal/conditional R^2 (MuMIn::r.squaredGLMM), and standardized coefficients via helper function.
7) Exploratory: repeat separate models for ave_writing, ave_reading, ave_listening with identical covariates and random effects. Apply FDR correction across the three domain tests.
8) Compare the sign and significance of bilingual coefficient against the original focal claim; report standardized effect sizes to address scale differences.
9) Document deviations (no cognitive ability, no German proficiency, different scaling, multilevel structure) and conduct sensitivity checks (e.g., reweight filtering FSW_*_TR>0 subsets).
planned_method.models: Linear mixed-effects regression (lmerTest/lme4) with random intercepts for schools nested in countries: outcome ~ bilingual + controls + (1 | country_id/school_id).
planned_method.outcome_variable: average_english (mean of ave_writing, ave_reading, ave_listening), where each domain is averaged from PV1–PV5 plausible values.
planned_method.independent_variables: Primary: bilingual indicator (1=bilingual/mixed home language use, 0=monolingual in instructional language).
planned_method.control_variables: Gender (SQt01i01), age (I08_ST_A_S02A, centered), SES (HISEI, centered), parental education (PARED, z-score), cultural capital (SQt21i01 recoded to numeric, z-score). Exclude English-at-home students (I03_ST_A_S27B==1).
planned_method.tools_software: R (lmerTest, lme4 via lmerTest, dplyr, tidyverse, MuMIn, reshape).

planned_estimation_and_test.estimation: Fixed-effect coefficient on bilingual (unstandardized and standardized), and marginal/conditional R^2.
planned_estimation_and_test.test: Satterthwaite t-tests for fixed effects from lmerTest; two-sided p-values. For domain models, apply Benjamini–Hochberg FDR correction across three tests.

planned_method.missing_data_handling: Listwise deletion at model fit (default na.omit in lmer). Exclude observations with zero/absent test weights per domain as in code (FSW_*_TR > 0).
planned_method.multiple_testing_policy: For exploratory domain-specific (writing, reading, listening) models, control FDR at 5% using BH correction.
planned_method.inference_criteria: Primary confirmation if bilingual coefficient is positive and statistically significant at alpha=0.05 (two-sided) in the overall model; secondary confirmation via consistency of sign across domains and positive standardized effect (>0).

files.Replication attempt code (FINAL).R: Main analysis script. Reads the dataset from /app/data/Final replication dataset.rds; constructs bilingual indicator (based on I03_ST_A_S26A), excludes English-at-home (I03_ST_A_S27B==1), computes English domain averages and an overall average_english, recodes cultural capital (SQt21i01) and centers/scales controls (age, HISEI, PARED). Fits a three-level mixed model with random intercepts for schools nested in countries: average_english ~ bilingual + gender + c_age + c_HISEI + Z_Parental + Z_Cultural + (1|country_id/school_id). Reports summary, R^2 (MuMIn), and standardized coefficients via helper function. Also fits domain-specific models (writing, reading, listening).
files.Final replication dataset.rds: Replication dataset containing English plausible values (PV* variables), background variables (HISEI, PARED, books SQt21i01, gender SQt01i01, age I08_ST_A_S02A), home language indicators (I03_ST_A_S26A, I03_ST_A_S27B), and IDs (country_id, school_id, respondent_id).

codebase.notes: I updated file paths to /app/data in the R script to comply with the required container execution environment. Dependencies include lmerTest (depends on lme4), dplyr/tidyverse, MuMIn, and reshape. The script prints results to console; no files are written. The bilingual classification line uses an R idiom that effectively treats value==1 as monolingual; categories 2 or 3 are treated as bilingual. Variable availability has been verified in the RDS (get_dataset_info/summary).

analysis.instructions: 1) Place Final replication dataset.rds in ./data so it is mounted at /app/data inside the container. 2) Start the R environment from the specified Docker image and ensure required R packages are installed. 3) Run the script Replication attempt code (FINAL).R. 4) Inspect console outputs for the mixed model summary, r.squaredGLMM, and standardized coefficients. 5) Record the bilingual coefficient sign, magnitude, SE, p-value, and standardized estimate for the overall model and domain models. 6) Apply FDR correction to the three domain p-values if reporting them. 7) Compare findings to the original claim, documenting deviations (no general cognitive ability or German proficiency variable).
analysis.comparison_metrics: Primary: sign and statistical significance of the bilingual coefficient (expected positive) in the adjusted model. Secondary: standardized effect size of bilingual (std. coefficient) for comparability across scales; marginal R^2 to contextualize variance explained; robustness across domains (writing/reading/listening).
